required.title: [Book] Cooking Class: 57 Fun Recipes Kids Will Love to Make (and Eat!)
required.author: Deanna F. Cook

required.title: [Read Aloud / Look Together] How Did That Get in My Lunchbox? The Story of Food
required.author: Chris Butterworth

suggested.title: [Book] Pretend Soup and Other Real Recipes: A Cookbook for Preschoolers and Up
suggested.author: Mollie Katzen

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Right This Very Minute: A Table-to-Farm Book
suggested.author: Lisl H. Detlefsen

suggested.title: [Activity] Visit a farmer's market, farm, or community garden

supplementary.title: [Video] YouTube: SciShow Kids — "Where Does Food Come From?" series

competence_markers.description: The student can explain where at least five common foods come from before they reach a store (the actual source — farm, animal, water — not "the store")
The student washes hands before food preparation without being reminded
The student can prepare at least three simple meals (breakfast, lunch, snack) with minimal supervision
The student follows kitchen safety rules consistently — awareness of hot surfaces, proper knife handling awareness, clean workspace
The student has tried at least ten new foods during this packet and can describe their flavors in their own words
The student can name foods that grow in at least two different seasons in their region
The student cleans up their workspace after food preparation without being told
The student is comfortable and confident in the kitchen — treats it as a workspace they belong in, not a place they visit

cross_connections.description: Fire is the oldest cooking tool. The student who understands fire as a survival skill sees the kitchen stove in context — it is a controlled fire, used to transform food.
Where food comes from at the production level — gardening, soil, livestock. 2.3.1 introduces the concept; 2.5 develops the ability to grow it yourself.
Cooking is applied chemistry. Watching bread rise, observing what heat does to an egg, noticing how salt changes flavor — these are scientific observations happening in the kitchen every day.
Cleaning up after yourself, following safety rules every time, preparing food to a standard — these are daily discipline practices.
Measuring cups, teaspoons, fractions of ingredients, counting eggs — cooking is applied math for young students.
Kitchen cleanliness is the overlap between cooking competence and sanitation. A clean kitchen is a safe kitchen.

content.why_this_matters:
Every day, somebody feeds you. Somebody decides what you will eat, goes to a store or garden to get it, brings it home, and turns it into a meal. That is real work — and most of it happens so quietly that you might not even notice it.

But here is something important: food does not start at the store. Before it sits on a shelf wrapped in plastic, food is alive. It grows in soil, swims in water, walks on land, or hangs from a tree. A chicken breast was once a chicken. A carrot was once a seed in the ground. An apple grew on a tree that someone planted years ago. When you understand where food actually comes from, you start to see the world differently. You realize that eating is connected to farms, animals, weather, seasons, and the work of thousands of people you will never meet.

You are also going to start working in the kitchen — not as a visitor, but as someone who belongs there. You will learn to handle real food, use real tools, and make real things to eat. The kitchen is a workspace, and you are going to become comfortable in it.

By the end of this packet, you will be able to prepare simple foods on your own, understand where your food comes from before it reaches the store, and know the basic rules that keep everyone safe in a kitchen.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Understanding of where food comes from** — farms, animals, gardens, water, soil — the chain from nature to plate
2. **Basic kitchen presence and comfort** — the student is at home in a kitchen, not a bystander
3. **Simple food preparation skills** — washing, peeling, measuring, mixing, spreading, assembling
4. **Kitchen safety fundamentals** — sharp objects, hot surfaces, hand washing, clean workspace
5. **Developing a palate** — trying new foods, tasting carefully, understanding flavors
6. **Seasonal awareness** — the basic idea that food grows in seasons, not all food is available all the time everywhere
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Where Does Food Come From?

Walk into any grocery store and you see rows of food in neat packages — bread in bags, chicken in trays, milk in cartons, apples in piles. It looks like food just appears there. It does not.

**From the ground:** Most of the food you eat started as a plant growing in soil. Wheat grows in fields — tall golden stalks — and is harvested, milled into flour, and baked into bread. Rice grows in flooded fields called paddies, mostly in Asia. Potatoes grow underground. Tomatoes grow on vines. Corn grows on stalks taller than most adults. Every fruit and vegetable you have ever eaten was once a living plant, connected to the earth by roots, fed by sunlight and water.

**From animals:** Chicken, beef, pork, fish, eggs, milk, cheese, butter — these all come from animals. A glass of milk came from a cow that was milked that morning or the day before. An egg was laid by a hen. The bacon at breakfast came from a pig. This is not a sad fact to hide from — it is how food works. People who eat meat should understand that an animal's life was part of the process. People who choose not to eat meat should understand why others do.

**From water:** Fish and seafood come from rivers, lakes, and oceans — caught by fishermen, or raised in fish farms. Seaweed grows in the ocean and is eaten in many cultures around the world.

**From gardens:** Some people grow their own food. A backyard garden can produce tomatoes, peppers, lettuce, herbs, beans, squash, and much more. A single tomato plant can produce dozens of tomatoes in one summer. Growing food yourself is one of the oldest human skills.

### Part 2: Seasons and Food

Here is something the grocery store hides from you: not all food grows all the time.

Strawberries grow in late spring and early summer. Apples ripen in fall. Pumpkins and squash come in autumn. Asparagus appears in spring. In winter, very little grows outdoors in most of the United States.

The reason you can buy strawberries in January is that they were grown far away — in California, Mexico, or South America — and shipped thousands of miles in refrigerated trucks. This is a modern miracle of transportation. But it means the strawberries you eat in January traveled farther than most people travel in a year, and they were picked before they were ripe so they would survive the journey. That is why they often taste less sweet than the strawberries you can pick in June.

When you eat food that is in season and grown nearby, it usually tastes better, costs less, and traveled a shorter distance to reach you. Knowing what is in season where you live is the beginning of food awareness.

### Part 3: The Kitchen Is a Workspace

A kitchen is not a living room. It is a workspace — like a workshop or a garage — where real things get made using real tools. Some of those tools are sharp. Some of them are hot. All of them deserve respect.

**Your place in the kitchen:** You belong here. Not as a guest who watches from a stool, but as someone who works. You will wash vegetables, peel carrots, measure flour, crack eggs, stir pots, spread butter, assemble sandwiches, and eventually cook meals on your own. Every time you enter the kitchen to help prepare food, you are building a skill you will use for the rest of your life.

**The rules of the workspace:**

1. **Wash your hands before you touch food.** Every time. With soap. For at least 20 seconds. This is the single most important food safety rule.
2. **Clean as you go.** Put away ingredients when you are done with them. Wipe up spills immediately. A cluttered workspace leads to mistakes.
3. **Knives are tools, not toys.** You will learn to use a knife when your mentor decides you are ready. Until then, you watch how knives are used and you never touch them without permission. When you do learn, you will learn correctly — how to hold the knife, how to hold the food, where your fingers go.
4. **Hot things stay hot.** Stoves, ovens, pots, and pans can burn you. You learn where hot things are and you stay aware. Pot handles point inward so they do not get bumped. You never reach across a hot burner. You use oven mitts, not towels that can slip.
5. **Ask before you taste.** Raw eggs, raw meat, and some raw ingredients can make you sick. Always ask your mentor before tasting something you are not sure about.

### Part 4: Simple Food You Can Make

You do not need to be a chef to make real food. Start here:

**Breakfast foods you can learn to make:**
- Toast with butter or peanut butter (using a toaster safely)
- A bowl of oatmeal (measuring oats and water, microwaving or stovetop with supervision)
- Scrambled eggs (with supervision at the stove — cracking eggs is a skill worth practicing)
- Fruit salad (washing, peeling, and cutting soft fruit with a butter knife or child-safe knife)
- Yogurt with granola and fruit (assembly — no cooking required)

**Lunch foods you can learn to make:**
- Sandwiches — the foundation of independence. Choose bread, choose fillings, spread, assemble, cut. A child who can make their own sandwich can feed themselves.
- Quesadillas — tortilla, cheese, fillings, fold, heat in a pan with supervision
- Salad — washing greens, tearing lettuce, adding toppings, making simple dressing (oil, vinegar, salt)

**Snacks you can prepare:**
- Ants on a log (celery, peanut butter, raisins)
- Apple slices with cheese or nut butter
- Vegetables with hummus (washing and cutting soft vegetables)
- Trail mix (measuring and mixing nuts, dried fruit, seeds)

### Part 5: Tasting and Trying

Your job is to try things. Not to love everything — nobody loves every food. But to try, honestly, with an open mouth and an open mind.

Some foods taste strange the first time. That is normal. Research shows that many children need to try a food eight to fifteen times before they develop a taste for it. The first time you tried cheese, or olives, or fish, or spinach, you may not have liked it. That does not mean you will never like it. Your palate — your sense of taste — grows and changes as you do.

**Rules for tasting:**
- Try one real bite. Not a lick. A bite.
- You do not have to finish it if you genuinely do not like it. But you do have to try it honestly.
- Describe what you taste. Is it sweet? Sour? Salty? Bitter? Crunchy? Soft? Using words for flavors builds your awareness.
- Try it again another time. Tastes change. Foods you hated at five you may love at eight.

Being a picky eater is not a personality. It is a stage. You move through it by trying things — repeatedly, honestly, and without drama.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Food Origin Understanding
**Developing well:** The child can explain where common foods come from with specificity — "Bread is made from flour, which is made from wheat that grows in fields." They connect the food on their plate to farms, animals, or gardens. They notice labels at the grocery store and ask where things come from.

**Developing slowly:** The child's answer to "where does milk come from?" is "the store." They have not connected food to its sources despite reading and discussion.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you done the food origin tracking activity? Have you visited a farm or farmers' market? Abstract understanding often requires a concrete experience to click.

### 2. Kitchen Safety Habits
**Developing well:** Handwashing before food prep is automatic. The child is aware of hot surfaces and sharp objects without being afraid of them. They clean up without being asked. Safety habits are becoming routine rather than requiring reminders.

**Developing slowly:** You remind them to wash hands every time. They reach across hot surfaces carelessly. They leave a mess and walk away.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling these habits consistently yourself? Children absorb the standard they see, not the standard they are told about.

### 3. Food Preparation Independence
**Developing well:** The child can prepare the three required simple meals with you present but not helping. They gather ingredients, measure, assemble, and clean up. They are confident and treat the task as normal rather than special.

**Developing slowly:** The child needs step-by-step instruction for tasks they have done before. They wait for you to tell them what to do next rather than working through the process.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you stepping back far enough? The most common barrier to independence is a mentor who continues to direct every step out of habit. Stand back. Let them figure out the sequence. Intervene only for safety.

### 4. Palate Development
**Developing well:** The child tries new foods without drama. They can describe flavors in their own words. Their taste journal shows honest engagement — "I did not like the olive but it was salty and sort of oily" is much better than "Gross."

**Developing slowly:** The child refuses to try new foods or makes a minimal token effort. The taste journal has one-word entries or is empty.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the environment around new foods calm and pressure-free? Are you trying new foods alongside them? Children who see adults trying unfamiliar foods and describing them honestly are far more likely to do the same.
